title: All-Electric Quantum State Transfer via Spin-Orbit Phase Matching
authors: Madhumita Sarkar, Roopayan Ghosh, Charles G. Smith, Maksym Myronov, Sougato Bose
abstract: Semiconductor hole-spin qubits offer a promising route to quantum computation due to their weak hyperfine interaction, and strong intrinsic spin-orbit coupling enabling electric control of qubits. Scalable architectures, however, require coherent long-distance quantum state transfer, which is hindered in these systems by spin-orbit induced anisotropic exchange. Here we show that this limitation can be overcome by using an all-electric control protocol. By tuning the electric field strength, we identify discrete spin-orbit phase-matching conditions that restore near-perfect state transfer, independent of the rotation axis. Complementarily, controlling the electric field direction aligns the spin-orbit axis, suppressing excitation non-conserving processes and enabling robust transfer without fine tuning. Our results establish that electrical control of spin-orbit phases through either magnitude tuning or axis alignment as a practical route for robust quantum information transport in hole-spin quantum dot arrays.
summary: This paper proposes an all-electric method to achieve high-fidelity quantum state transfer in germanium hole-spin qubit arrays. By precisely tuning the electric field to match spin-orbit phases or align the spin-orbit axis, the detrimental effects of anisotropic exchange can be neutralized. This provides a scalable pathway for quantum information transport in semiconductor-based quantum architectures.
main_problem: Achieving high-fidelity, long-distance quantum state transfer in semiconductor hole-spin qubit arrays, which is typically hindered by spin-orbit induced anisotropic exchange.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that all-electric control via tuning the electric field strength (phase-matching) or direction (axis alignment) can restore near-perfect state transfer and suppress leakage processes.
method: The study uses a Schrieffer-Wolff transformation to derive an effective spin Hamiltonian from a Fermi-Hubbard model and employs Van Vleck perturbation theory to analyze the low-energy dynamics and robustness against charge noise.
model_or_system: A chain of semiconductor hole-spin qubits (specifically Ge hole-spin quantum dots) acting as a quantum bus, modeled by an anisotropic exchange Hamiltonian with spin-orbit rotation.
key_observables: State-transfer fidelity (F), effective tunneling amplitude (teff), and oscillation frequency.
important_parameters: Spin-orbit rotation angle (thetas), spin-orbit axis orientation (nso), electric field strength (E), and magnetic field (B).
main_assumptions: The system operates in the strongly interacting regime (U >> t) and assumes quasi-static charge noise as the primary decoherence mechanism.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows state-transfer fidelity as a function of the spin-orbit angle, highlighting discrete high-fidelity peaks at commensurate points.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of SOC-induced anisotropy, presents an effective Hamiltonian model, proposes two electric control protocols (magnitude and axis tuning), evaluates the robustness of these protocols against magnetic fields and charge noise, and concludes with a scalable 2D architecture proposal.
why_interesting: This work is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum networks as it provides a mechanism to use intrinsic spin-orbit coupling as a resource for robust quantum communication rather than a source of decoherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Pictorial representation of our setup for quantum communication. The intermediate qubits shown in blue rep- resents the quantum bus. The other two spins shown in red at the ends (Q1, Q2) represents the qubits of interest.

FIG. 2. (a)-(c) Maximum state-transfer fidelity (evaluated within a finite time window t ≤10 µs) as a function of the spin-orbit rotation angle θso for different orientations of the spin-orbit axis nso, shown with different colours as indicated in the leftmost plot. For all system sizes L = 4, 6, 10, per- fect state transfer is achieved at discrete commensurate val- ues θso = 2πn

FIG. 3. State-transfer fidelity F(t) as a function of time for different spin–orbit configurations. (a) Comparison of dy- namics for different spin–orbit axes. In the isotropic Heisen- berg limit (θso = 0), the fidelity exhibits clean periodic os- cillations approaching unity (blue), corresponding to ideal coherent state transfer. For a generic tilted spin–orbit axis nso = [0.5, 0.5, 0.707] with θso = 0.3π, anisotropic exchange components suppress the fidelity due to mixing of excitation sectors (green). In contrast, aligning the spin–orbit axis along the z-direction, nso = [0, 0, 1], restores high-fidelity oscilla- tions despite finite spin-orbit coupling (red), demonstrating that...

FIG. 4. Maximum state-transfer fidelity within a chosen coherence window (t ≤10) as a function of the spin-orbit axis components nx and nz (with |nso| = 1) for two representative rotation angles: (a) θ = 0.4π, (b) θ = 0.55π. These results are for L = 4. In both cases, the fidelity is maximized when the spin-orbit axis aligns close to the z-direction.

FIG. 5. (a,b) Time evolution of the fidelity F(t) for moderate (B = 50 MHz) and large (B = 1000 MHz) magnetic fields in the optimal axis-alignment regime. For experimentally relevant fields (B = 50 MHz), the dynamics remain essentially identical to the zero-field case, while larger fields introduce visible deviations only at longer times. In both cases, however, the maximum fidelity remains unchanged, demonstrating that the axis-alignment protocol is robust against variations in magnetic- field strength.
